schools.summary: Henry reads Josiah's national assembly as a doomed king doing his duty and leaving the event to God, making the reformation a corporate act to avert corporate judgment.

Keil notes the prophets (perhaps Jeremiah and Zephaniah) gathered to renew the covenant, that Chronicles names Levites instead, and that 'he read' means he had it read; Ellicott prefers the reading 'there gathered' and takes the elders as the nation's representatives.
Gill: Josiah sent through the land and convened all the principal men at Jerusalem.
Clarke frames the assembly as the opening act of the whole sweeping reform the chapter records.

Ellicott takes 'the prophets' as the prophetic order (rejecting the Targum's anachronistic 'scribes'), calls the crowd a natural hyperbole, and is cautious whether the king himself read; Keil sets him by the pillar reading the book of the covenant.
Clarke finds it singular that the king, not the priests, read, and takes him for a mediator; Wesley agrees he read it himself, unashamed like Solomon preaching or David keeping the door.
Gill notes only that Josiah went up to the temple from his palace.

debates.question: Did Josiah himself read the book of the covenant?

sides.position: The king read it himself, as a mediator between God and the people
Not necessarily the king; the priests were charged to read the law

schools.summary: Clarke lays out the covenant in three articles (no other gods, the law for a rule, the whole heart engaged); Wesley reads the people's standing as their consent, and commends binding ourselves to duty with solemnity.
Ellicott stresses 'the covenant' so often broken, and 'stood to' as 'entered'; Keil takes the people to have actually come into the covenant, not merely continued in an old one.
Gill identifies the pillar with Solomon's brazen scaffold, the king's appointed station.

Keil takes 'second order' as the common priests and 'keepers of the door' as the door-keeping Levites, the eradication already begun before the covenant; Ellicott prefers the singular 'priest of the second rank' (the deputy) and finds the sending of ashes to Bethel a mark of textual corruption.
Clarke: the disused vessels must be destroyed lest they tempt to idolatry again; Wesley reads 'the grove' as its image and the ashes carried to Bethel as studied contempt for idolatry's chief seat.
Henry marvels at the depth of idolatry found even in the temple after eighteen godly years, and grounds the whole riddance in the law of Deut. 7:5.
Gill takes 'the second order' of the two chief priests next the high priest, of the lines of Eleazar and Ithamar.

debates.question: Who are 'the priests of the second order'?

sides.position: The common priests, as distinguished from the high priest
The high priest's deputy (singular), or the heads of the twenty-four courses

debates.question: Why were the ashes carried to Bethel?

sides.position: A deliberate act of contempt, to pollute idolatry's chief seat and leave no trace in the kingdom
The text appears corrupt; it is strange only the vessels' ashes, not the idols', were sent there

themes: vessels of Baal and the host of heaven

schools.summary: Keil takes the Chemarim as non-Levitical priests the kings appointed for the high places (cf. Hosea 10:5; Zephaniah 1:4), distinct from the open Baal- and star-worshippers; Ellicott agrees they are unlawful Jehovah-priests and reads mazzaloth as the zodiac.
Clarke derives Chemarim from a root meaning scorched/black and tartly rebukes black clerical dress; Wesley takes them for the highest rank of priests, burning incense.
Gill: black-clothed priests, over against the Lord's priests in white linen.

sides.position: Black-robed priests, from a root meaning scorched or blackened
Doubtful it means black-robed, since priests wore white; perhaps 'weaver of spells'

themes: sun, moon and the host of heaven

schools.summary: Keil traces the Asherah image to Manasseh (removed, then restored by Amon), burned and ground to powder in the long-defiled Kidron; Ellicott reads 'graves of the sons of the people' as the common graves, a mark of utter contempt.
Clarke identifies the grove as Asherah cast on the common burial-place; Wesley glosses 'the people' as that idolatrous people, per the Chronicler.
Gill takes 'the grove' for a carved image of Ashtoreth/Astarte, not a grove of trees.

themes: the Asherah out of the temple

schools.summary: Keil takes the Kedeshim's houses for tents in the temple court where women wove tent-shrines for Asherah; Ellicott calls them male prostitutes (perhaps eunuchs) and 'the last infamy of Canaanite nature-worship' set in the sanctuary itself.
Clarke takes the word for all kinds of prostitutes and the weaving for tent-coverings of the impure rites; Wesley grounds sodomy in idol-worship and canvasses what the women wove.
Gill explains the unnatural sin done in the idols' honour and notes Abarbanel's and the Targum's alternative readings.

outliers.note: Reports Abarbanel's alternative that the Kedeshim were idolatrous priests reckoned 'holy,' and the Targum's reading 'houses of things consecrated to idols,' rather than sodomites.

schools.summary: Keil takes the priests as Levitical, summoned to Jerusalem so they cannot minister illegally, the high places defiled from Geba to Beersheba (whole kingdom); Ellicott explains the gate-altars as offered at by those entering the city and confesses 'Joshua' unknown.
Wesley reads the gate-altars as raised to tutelary gods and the governor's name recorded to show Josiah's impartiality; Clarke takes the gate of Joshua as where the governor heard causes.
Gill takes the priests brought out to be Aaron's sons who had served in the high places.

themes: the high places of the gates

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the high-place priests were barred from the altar as incapacitated by their illegal ministry, yet allowed to live on the offerings among their brethren, like priests with a bodily defect (Lev. 21:17).
Clarke treats them as priests with an infirmity, kept by right to their support; Wesley calls their exclusion a just punishment yet Josiah's provision a mitigation.
Gill: removed from the high places, they were not admitted to the altar, having offered in forbidden places.
Keil derives Topheth from a root meaning to spit (an object of abhorrence) and links Gehinnom to Gehenna in Matt. 5:22; Ellicott records both derivations and takes Molech as another form of melech.
Gill and Wesley derive Topheth from toph, a drum beaten to drown the children's cries; Clarke cites Jerome that it was once a pleasant spot, later a type of hell.
Gill: Josiah defiled it by casting filth into it, in contempt of the child-sacrifice practised there.

debates.question: What is the meaning or origin of 'Topheth'?

sides.position: From toph, a drum, beaten to drown the burning children's cries
From a root meaning to spit/spit out — 'the abomination'

outliers.note: Questions the lurid tradition that Topheth was a public shambles where criminals' limbs were quartered by thousands: 'on what authority do such descriptions rest?'

schools.summary: Keil argues for living horses (only 'cleared away,' while the chariots were burned), sacred to the sun and received from Upper Asia; Ellicott fixes their place by Nathan-melech's cell in the porticoes and links them to the sun's processions.

Clarke reports Jarchi that adorers mounted the horses at sunrise and leaves it open whether living or sculptured; Wesley takes them consecrated to the sun's swiftness, to be sacrificed or to draw the chariots.
Gill insists they were real living horses given by Manasseh and Amon, marshalling classical proof of horses sacred to the sun.
Henry laments that horses and chariots meant for Israel's defence were squandered on the sun.

debates.question: Were the horses given to the sun living animals or images?

sides.position: Living horses, kept for the sun's worship

schools.summary: Keil dates the rooftop altars to Ahaz, restored by Manasseh and Amon and dedicated to the host of heaven (Zeph. 1:5; Jer. 19:13); Ellicott finds in the verb a vivid 'ran from thence,' marking the haste of the work.
Gill notes the flat Judean roofs bore altars, these on the palace roof for daily incense to the sun.
Wesley reads the housetop altars as a mark of how mad the people were on their idols, the dust cast into Kidron to show detestation.

Keil sets Solomon's high places on the southern Mount of Olives (1 Kings 11:7), defiled and filled with bones; Ellicott derives 'mount of corruption' possibly from 'anointing' (the olive oil) and doubts Solomon's own chapels survived Hezekiah.
Clarke traces the name from mount of unction to mount of corruption for its idolatry; Wesley holds these were Manasseh's or Amon's altars in Solomon's place, branded with his name as a rebuke.
Gill takes the high places 'before Jerusalem' to include those within, at the gates, and without the city.

debates.question: Were 'the high places which Solomon built' his actual structures?

sides.position: Not Solomon's own, but later altars by Manasseh/Amon in the same place, branded with his name
Probably only sacred rock-sites, since Solomon's chapels would not have survived Hezekiah's reform

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the pair 'pillars and asherahs' and notes 'their place' is a technical term for an idol's site; Keil treats the verse as a closer definition of the defiling just named.
Clarke: filling the places with men's bones was the utmost defilement possible; Wesley draws the symmetry of ashes to graves and bones to the idols' sites, so idolatry is loathsome both ways.
Gill takes the images broken to be Ashtoreth, Chemosh and Milcom, reset by Manasseh or Amon.

Keil takes 'the high place' in apposition to 'the altar' at Bethel, burned and pulverized, the golden calf already carried off by Assyria (Hosea 10:5-6); Ellicott confirms the calf's absence as an indirect fulfilment of Hosea and wonders how a stone structure was burned.
Wesley explains Josiah's reach into Bethel: the city was now under Judah, added long before by Abijah, the calf gone but no remains to be left.
Gill: the altar and high place Jeroboam made for the calf-worship.
Henry marks how a king from Judah executed what the man of God from Judah had foretold, then kept the passover.

Keil reads the burning of the graves' bones on the altar as fulfilling the prophecy of 1 Kings 13:2 while sparing the prophet's own tomb; Ellicott judges the Hebrew defective, a scribe's eye slipping from one 'man of God' to the other, and prefers the fuller LXX.
Clarke supplies the fuller Septuagint/Syriac text of the scene; Wesley marvels that Josiah saw it done with his own eyes and that the words were spoken three hundred years before.
Gill notes Josiah turned from the altar and saw the sepulchres himself.
Ellicott renders the 'title' a monument or memorial stone (cf. Ezek. 39:15; Jer. 31:21) and points back to the burial in 1 Kings 13:29; Keil takes the ziyyun for a sepulchral stone.
Clarke: a stone, image, or inscription which the old prophet took care to make remarkable.
Gill sees a conspicuous inscribed pillar, kept up in later times, which preserved both prophets' bones from the fire.

outliers.note: Records, but sets little store by, the Jewish tradition (Kimchi) that nettles grew on one side of the grave and fragrant herbs on the other.

themes: the man of God from Judah

schools.summary: Ellicott sharpens both verbs ('Let him rest'; 'suffered his bones to escape' disturbance) and explains 'came out of Samaria' as marking the old prophet a citizen of the northern kingdom.
Gill: the man of God's bones were not to be taken from the grave as the others were.
Clarke refers the reader to 1 Kings 13:31, where the old prophet asked to be buried beside the man of God.

themes: the deceiver spared for the faithful
God's difference kept in the dust

schools.summary: Keil reports Josiah destroyed the Samaria high-place houses as at Bethel; Ellicott (from 2 Chron. 34:6) extends the reach to Naphtali and offers political grounds for Josiah's authority beyond his borders.
Clarke grounds the reach in sacred right: Israel kingless, Josiah of the blood royal with a divine commission over all the inheritance of Jacob.
Gill takes 'the houses of the high places' for the idol-temples and their priests' dwellings.

debates.question: By what right did Josiah reform beyond his own borders in Samaria?

sides.position: A divine commission and his royal claim to the whole inheritance of Jacob
Political — as an Assyrian vassal, or by the remnant's sympathy, or amid Assyria's imminent collapse

themes: a son of David over the north

schools.summary: Ellicott marks the stronger verb 'he slaughtered' and the contrast with the spared Judean Levites, these being heathenish priests under Deut. 17:2; Keil notes the priests offered on their own altars and bones burned to defile them.
Clarke: their lives, as corrupters of the people, were forfeited to the law; Wesley sees the fulfilment of 1 Kings 13:2 and explains the returned Israelites' revived idolatry.
Gill distinguishes the slain Baal-priests from the spared incense-priests of the true God.

Ellicott renders 'Hold a passover' and cites Hezekiah's precedent (2 Chron. 30:1), correcting the phrase to 'this book of the covenant'; Keil notes the brief account, the whole nation's part, and the strict Mosaic conformity.
Gill: the command reached not Jerusalem only but the whole kingdom.
Henry contrasts Jehu, who only ceased to do evil, with Josiah, who learned to do well by keeping up the instituted ordinances (Lev. 18:30).
Keil names the distinctions — all the nation joined, kept by strict Mosaic law; Ellicott makes the two verses a parenthesis underlining 'as it is written in this book of the covenant.'
Clarke insists the uniqueness lay in purer principles and spirit, not numbers; Wesley enlarges on the care, sacrifices and joy, sharpened by the memory of Manasseh and Amon.
Gill takes 'the days of the judges' with Chronicles to mean since Samuel, the last judge.

themes: no such passover since the judges

schools.summary: Ellicott, following Ewald, takes the two verses to mean the passover was never so kept before, especially in its added offerings (Deut. 16:2) and unity of place (Deut. 16:5), dismissing the notion that no passover had ever been held.
Gill reckons from the date how early in the eighteenth year the reformation must have begun, the same year the temple was repaired and the law found.

Ellicott traces the turn to private superstition (necromancers, teraphim, 'dunglings'), weighting 'that were spied' as idols searched out and hidden ones consumed (Deut. 18:9); Keil makes the whole aim to establish the words of the found book.
Clarke takes 'the images' for teraphim (Gen. 31:19); Wesley reads the heaped words as showing all instruments of idolatry destroyed, and 'spied' as all discovered even when hidden.
Gill: the workers with familiar spirits and wizards were never to be allowed among Israel.

themes: to establish the words of the law

schools.summary: Ellicott sees no strict contradiction with the like praise of Hezekiah, yet grants the law was never so rigorously kept, and cites Jer. 22:15 for Josiah's righteous judging; Keil hears in the verse the very approach of doom.
Henry unfolds the fourfold praise (turned to the Lord, with heart and soul, with all his might, by the whole law of Moses) and frames the coming ruin as a mystery of providence.
Clarke ranks him morally above even David — 'a greater but not a better man than Josiah.'
Gill reconciles the like praise of Hezekiah by allowing each king excelled in different things.

themes: praise on the edge of doom

schools.summary: Wesley: the judgment was delayed for Josiah's lifetime, but the corrupt people, secretly averse, returned to their idols as soon as he was gone, and Manasseh's unrepented sins were justly visited; Clarke calls them fickle and radically depraved, merely respited.
Ellicott reads 'the great heat of his wrath' and points to Manasseh's provocations foretold by Jeremiah and Zephaniah.
Gill: the people turned not with their whole heart but feignedly, so the wrath was not withdrawn.

themes: a real king, a hollow people

schools.summary: Keil reads 'I will put away Judah as I put away Israel' (cf. 2 Kings 17:20) as a decree announced through Jeremiah and Zephaniah, not a warning to be averted.
Gill: 'out of my sight' means from God's special care, not his all-seeing eye — the irrevocable decree of captivity.
Wesley: the promise concerning the temple was conditional, and the broken conditions made God 'know his breach of promise' toward them.

themes: I will remove Judah as Israel

schools.summary: Keil looks through the closing formula to the catastrophe it prefaces, identifying Necho II of the Saite dynasty about to march against Assyria.
Gill glosses 'the rest of the acts' of the king's work in abolishing idolatry and restoring true worship.

Keil sets out Necho's march to the Euphrates with a full account of the Pharaoh; Ellicott weighs the chronology of Nineveh's fall to decide whether the 'king of Assyria' was Babylonian or truly Assyrian, and reconstructs the battle at Megiddo.

Clarke and Wesley take the 'king of Assyria' for Nabopolassar of Babylon, now head of the empire, the quarrel over Carchemish; Wesley reads 'slew him' as the death-wound and suspects Josiah's rashness.
Gill identifies Pharaoh-nechoh (the Targum's 'lame') as Herodotus' son of Psammetichus.

debates.question: Who was 'the king of Assyria' Necho marched against?

sides.position: The king of Babylon (Nabopolassar), now head of the former Assyrian empire
Possibly a true king of Assyria, if Nineveh had not yet fallen

themes: a war without a clear call

schools.summary: Keil fills out the second-chariot journey from the Chronicler and measures the grief by Zech. 12:11 and 2 Chron. 35:25; Ellicott notes Jehoahaz was not the eldest, chosen as the abler prince yet (per Jer. 22:10) mistakenly.
Clarke reads 'dead' as 'dying' — mortally wounded at Megiddo, dead at Jerusalem — with the mourning at Hadadrimmon; Wesley notes Jehoahaz younger than Jehoiakim yet preferred, the lion of Ezek. 19:3.
Gill: the servants moved the wounded king to another chariot, where he died on the way.
Keil identifies Jehoahaz with Jeremiah's Shallum, a three-month reign of evil, the people's hope disappointed, the violent young lion of Ezekiel 19:3.
Gill identifies him with the Shallum of Jeremiah's prophecy.
Clarke settles the chronology: Jehoahaz twenty-three and reigning three months, his brother Eliakim twenty-five and elder, so the people raised the younger, more martial son.
Henry sets the reign in Jerusalem's long shadow, the people preferring a warlike avenger to their father's reformation.
Wesley takes 'his fathers' as his grandparents Manasseh and Amon, whose idolatry he restored, undoing his father's work.
Ellicott hears the lament of Ezekiel 19:1, the young lion that 'devoureth men,' alluding to the king's rapacity.
Gill glosses the evil in a single word — idolatry.
Henry warns from the phrase 'all that his fathers had done' that Jehoahaz had chosen his patterns, and an error in that choice is fatal (Phil. 3:17).

themes: the way of Manasseh and Amon

schools.summary: Ellicott sets Riblah on the Orontes, has Necho seize Jehoahaz by fraud (Ezek. 19:4), corrects the Herodotus 'Kadytis' to Kadesh, and prefers the smaller tribute figures; Keil weighs 'that he might not reign' against 'when he had become king.'
Clarke reconstructs Jehoahaz's defeat and capture on Necho's return from Carchemish; Wesley gives the reasons for the binding — taking the crown without consent, or renewing the war.
Gill places Riblah in Hamath (Antioch, per Jerome) and supposes Jehoahaz had gone there with an army.

themes: the tribute of silver and gold

schools.summary: Keil reads the name-change as a sign of dependence (cf. Gen. 41:45; Dan. 1:7), noting the restraint of giving genuine Israelite names; Ellicott has the change mark Jehoiakim as Necho's creature and cites Jer. 22:10 for Jehoahaz's foretold death in Egypt.
Clarke reads Eliakim and Jehoiakim as of the same import, the change asserting Necho's supremacy, like Mattaniah to Zedekiah; Wesley calls the naming an act of lordship.
Gill: Eliakim was made king in the room of Josiah, not Jehoahaz, whom Necho allowed no lawful right.
Keil looks ahead over the eleven years, drawing from Jer. 22:13 and Josephus the character of an oppressive, gain-grasping, blood-shedding prince.
Clarke: Necho set Jehoiakim as viceroy to collect taxes, assessed in proportion but, he fears, not fairly by this bad king.
Gill: Jehoiakim gave Pharaoh the hundred talents of silver and the talent of gold laid on the land.
Henry marks the reversal — once Israel spoiled the Egyptians, now the Egyptians spoil Israel — 'see what woeful changes sin makes.'

themes: the tribute wrung from the people

schools.summary: Keil frames the reign — eleven years in the spirit of ungodly forefathers — and notes the mother Zebudah of Rumah.
Gill fixes from the age that Jehoiakim was two years older than the deposed Jehoahaz, the elder passed over by the people.
Keil registers the formula, having already filled in from Jeremiah the oppression and violence of the reign.
Clarke calls Jehoiakim an unprincipled, oppressive tyrant, whose character Jeremiah (an eyewitness) draws in Jer. 22:13ff.
Gill refers 'all that his fathers had done' to Amon and Manasseh.
Henry: the tribute that made him poor did not make him good, yet the good work God begins by his Spirit cannot be undone.

themes: the idolatry of Manasseh and Amon
